summary: Conceptually solid lesson. Hits all six CHA topics, all three required examples, and at least four of the listed AP traps. The dx-vs-dy decision slide (13_axis_choice) is exactly the metacognitive scaffold students need. The pause prompt asking 'is it a disc or a washer?' is great. Two minor pedagogical gaps and one narration-length concern; none block shipping.

issues.description: Doesn't explicitly say the BASE region lives in the xy-plane and the cross-sections rise PERPENDICULAR to it. A student could conflate the base region (2D, between the curves) with the cross-section itself (also 2D, but rising out of the page). The custom hook slide (02_hook) can absorb this if the bread-slice visual is unambiguous, but the narration could add one sentence: 'The base region lives flat in the xy-plane; the slices rise up perpendicular to it.'

AP also commonly tests isoceles-right-triangle cross-sections (A = s^2/2 when s is a leg). Module doesn't mention. Acceptable since the three listed shapes (square, semicircle-with-diameter, equilateral triangle) cover the most-frequent FRQ setups. Optional add if room.

Narration is ~140 words including arithmetic computation of 256 pi / 5. Borderline over the 25-80 word target. At -3% rate this slide will run roughly 50+ seconds. Could be acceptable for a solution-reveal slide where the viewer is following along on paper. If the lesson-writer wants to trim, the antiderivative line can be compressed.

No worked example integrates in dy. Slide 13_axis_choice explains the rule, but the student never sees it executed. Acceptable trade-off given the 19-section budget and the four required examples; flagging only so the lesson-writer can drop a brief mention into 13_axis_choice narration ('we won't work a dy example today but the rule is the same — just flip your variable').
